narrative: I'm sorry to share this tough news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 69% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your low Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings—both in the bottom tier compared to the typical student's mid-range performance—as well as your undergraduate GPA of 2.10, well below the average of 3.21. Your LSAT score of 23.50 is also significantly lower than the dataset average of 36.70, and your family comes from a lower-middle income bracket, unlike many applicants from higher-earning backgrounds. As an Asian female planning to study full-time, you're in a smaller subset of applicants, with Asians making up just 5.9% and females 43.6% of the group, while whites and males dominate at 83.8% and 56.4%, respectively. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your Year 3 decile ranking, which strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure due to its low value. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which similarly contributed to the failure outlook because it fell so far short of what's typical. Your Year 1 decile followed closely, reinforcing the same concerning pattern of early underperformance relative to peers. Your racial background as Asian had a notable but slightly lesser impact, nudging the prediction toward failure in the context of the mostly white applicant pool. Family income and undergraduate GPA each played supporting roles, both pointing toward higher risk because they were below average. Gender and full-time status had the smallest influences, still tilting slightly against passing but not as decisively. Overall, your academic metrics stood out as much weaker than those of students who typically pass the bar, painting a picture of potential struggles ahead. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT with targeted prep to boost your score toward or above the average, build stronger study habits proven to help with bar success, and consider gaining relevant experience like internships that demonstrate resilience. Keep pushing forward—you have time to turn this around and show your potential.
